ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for claypot prawn glass noodles; closest defensible anchor is the generic noodle dish class. Prawn and glass noodle specificity not captured at FoodOn term level.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans and products thereof — cooked prawn-based dish with noodles
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers crustacean-based dishes; glass noodle component not separately coded. Closest parent match accepted as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a prawn-dominant noodle dish.

main_ingredients: Glass noodles (tang hoon / mung bean vermicelli)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard claypot serving of approximately 550 g, typically consumed as a complete main course for one person.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Nutrient Composition of Food: Prawn Glass Noodle Dish (composite estimate)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for '416D Claypot Prawn Tang Hoon' exists in the HPB Foodo database. Values are estimated from HPB Foodo component data for cooked prawns, mung bean glass noodles, fish sauce, soy sauce, sesame oil, and garlic-ginger aromatics, weighted by typical recipe proportions for a Singaporean claypot prawn glass noodle dish. Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are retained as the primary numeric basis and cross-checked against HPB component data for plausibility. All values are plausible for this dish type.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Mung bean glass noodles (tang hoon / cellophane noodles), cooked
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for claypot prawn glass noodles as a composite dish. GI of 45 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked mung bean glass noodles / cellophane noodles (GI approximately 39–50, reference glucose = 100), which is the dominant carbohydrate source in this dish. Prawn protein and fat components further blunt the glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as: (GI 45 × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (45 × 9.09 g/100g × 550 g / 100) / 100 = (45 × 49.995) / 100 ≈ 22.5. This is an analogous estimate; direct measurement of the composite dish is recommended for clinical precision.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 327 mg per 100 g, equivalent to approximately 1,800 mg sodium per full 550 g claypot serving, driven by fish sauce, soy sauce, and seasoning paste
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit this dish to a half-portion or request reduced fish sauce and soy sauce, as a full serving provides approximately 78% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glass noodles (mung bean starch) have a low-to-moderate GI of approximately 45, with 9.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g; prawn protein (6.4 g/100 g) moderates postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume a moderate portion of this dish, as the low-GI glass noodles and high protein content from prawns help blunt glycaemic response, but total carbohydrate load per full serving (~50 g) warrants portion control and pairing with non-starchy vegetables.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 3.64 g per 100 g and cholesterol at 45.45 mg per 100 g from prawns and cooking fats; sodium at 327 mg per 100 g adds cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake, request reduced oil and sauce, and note that prawns contribute dietary cholesterol (~250 mg per full serving) which may be relevant for those with LDL management goals.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat at 3.64 g per 100 g from cooking fats is pro-inflammatory; chilli and curry-style seasonings may aggravate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request reduced oil and omit chilli or curry paste where possible, as saturated fat and nightshade-derived compounds may exacerbate joint inflammation, while ginger and garlic in the dish provide mild anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Claypot Prawn Glass Noodles (砂煲虾冬粉, also known as Claypot Prawn Tang Hoon) is a popular Singaporean hawker and restaurant dish with Thai culinary influences, served hot directly in a clay or ceramic pot. The dish features succulent whole or halved prawns simmered together with mung bean glass noodles (tang hoon / cellophane noodles) in a savoury, aromatic broth seasoned with fish sauce, soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, and spring onion, sometimes with chilli or a mild curry-style paste for depth. The glass noodles absorb the flavourful broth during cooking, becoming silky, translucent, and tender with a slightly gelatinous texture, while the prawns remain juicy and sweet. The claypot retains heat exceptionally well, keeping the dish piping hot throughout the meal and imparting a subtle earthen aroma. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 124 kcal, 6.4 g protein, 6.9 g fat (3.6 g saturated), 9.1 g carbohydrate, 1.5 g sugar, 0.7 g dietary fibre, and 327 mg sodium. A full 550 g serving delivers approximately 680 kcal and 1,800 mg sodium. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), driven by the mung bean glass noodles which have a lower GI than rice noodles or white rice. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to manageable carbohydrate load with protein buffering, high concern for hypertension due to elevated sodium from multiple condiments, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat and cholesterol from prawns. It is suitable as a main course for lunch or dinner and is commonly found in Singaporean hawker centres, coffee shops, and Thai-Singaporean restaurants.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this composite dish exists in the HPB Foodo or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are retained from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, original confidence 0.7) and cross-checked for plausibility against HPB component data for cooked prawns and mung bean glass noodles. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for mung bean glass noodles. Confidence is set to medium reflecting the estimated rather than directly measured nutrient basis.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite claypot dish is not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central at the composite dish level as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish are not available in approved sources at the composite dish level as of 2024. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.09 g per 100 g from the input record; this low value is plausible for a dish using small amounts of cooking oil and is retained. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on mung bean glass noodles (cellophane noodles) from the University of Sydney GI Database; direct measurement of the composite dish has not been performed. 5. The input record describes the dish as Thai-influenced but the vendor (416D) operates in Singapore; cuisine is classified as Singaporean-Thai fusion and multilingual naming covers both Singaporean mandatory languages (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) and Thai script per G6 rules. 6. Halal status is marked true as the dish contains no pork or alcohol in its standard preparation; individual vendor halal certification should be verified independently. 7. Core macros (protein_g 6.36, fat_g 6.91, carbohydrate_g 9.09) are all non-zero and require no zero-macro justification.
